author: @HostileRespite
text: Line the outer rim with felt where the blades contact and that will reduce the nose significantly. Though, rubber is waterproof and should do well too so perhaps that is a better option. Some of the noise may be the blades hitting the support rods as well. A few small rubber bands down them may do the trick! Might be a bit time consuming putting them on, but hey, it'll be quieter! The very edge of the blades could use a bit of a curl upward, perhaps a half centemeter, just enough to help grab the wind easier as they come around to the "catch" side and cause the blade to swing out sooner.

author: @darkisland04
text: Who needs a scarecrow if one has 6-12 of these in a corn field? And the beauty of it is that one could generate electricity at the same time one is frightening the crows away! Actually someone in England in the early 20th century made such a gizmo. It had several metal arms which flapped in the breeze, and the contraption could also shoot off shotgun shells or firecrackers to scare the birds away.  Unfortunately, the metal arms were too heavy for any but the strongest winds, and someone had to be stationed at the field all the time to explode the shells or firecrackers. It never quite caught on!  This has MUCH more promise, I suspect! Great idea!

author: @crankfwd8209
text:
Love this, it reminds me of my Pringles tin turbine. Currently working on a bigger version and I have an idea that might help with your noise problem. I wanted to create a wind pocket instead of just the sail, as I think I could "trap" more air, so I needed a top and bottom cover. I've settled on the idea of using a folded material (card, plastic etc) like on an old Chinese hand fan. You could use the same idea but with folded rubber (like on an old bellows) and this would dampen any sound from the flaps and would also create a wind pocket, which I think is more efficient because you don't lose any air out the top or the bottom. 
Also, when you make the larger bladed version, you could mount it on a cylinder instead of a frame, as I feel you are losing "wind power" into the body of the open framework.
Please feel free to use my string and eyelet method, so as one flap opens, the previous one is pulled shut. This would create less drag on the closed side of the turbine and I think you would also get some benefit from the air being forced out of the closing flap. As always, thanks for posting this, you are the hardest working man on youtube.

author: @glencahalin4786
text:
I think, with the low Reynolds aerofoil one, that it'd be better to mount with the blades above the generator, that way it avoids blocking so much of the through wind, also causing turbulence, which matters to a lift style.

However with a drag style ( especially one with closing blades) as through wind if fairly irrelevant and it might be better to design that one so the generator is inside the blades, giving a more compact, in use, device but also lowering the centre of gravity and the leverage force that might cause it to fall over, essential no loss, or increase, of output but a more stable design.

author: @danedmiston9673
text:
I have more hope for your darrieus wind turbine.  About a 10 foot diameter rotor would be a good start.  A low Reynolds number airfoil of that width needs about 50 MPH of speed to start flying right.  The narrower the blade the more speed is needed to reach an acceptable Reynolds number.  At 5,000 almost nothing flies,  at 50,000 the low Reynolds number airfoils are at their lower limit, at 100,000  so-so,  At 1,000,000  great performance.   

Also more spacing between blades gives the wind more time to speed up again after the blade before it sucked the speed out of it.  Low Reynold number airfoils are popular on sailplanes and model airplanes because they don't need to be traveling at 100+ mph to get decent performance.

author: @thesentientneuron6550
text: Hey Rob! I have an interesting idea for you exploiting a fun thing in fluid mechanics. What if you replaced the flaps on your wind turbine with spinning rods? This would be a VAWT that exploits the Magnus effect to dynamically apply lift with minimal loss. The rotor assembly would thus spin in the opposite direction of the direction the rods are set to spin. And if you wanted to play with it further, you could use a planetary gearset to use the rotational energy of the rotor to spin the rods faster, enchancing the performance of the turbine. I think it can be made into a highly compact, portable form AND require even less skill to make. What are your thoughts?

author: @mattmill30
text:
since the lantern blades harness energy from the wind passing around the turbine, rather than passing through the turbine as is harnessed by airfoil, what if you attach the lantern upside-down (as seen at 5:00) with the airfoil mounted on-top?
I assume a single unit of the two combined would have negligible performance improvement.

author: @YahDude
text: Would the blades slamming back not cause more harm than good? I feel like left open it would be far more effecient. It would be interesting to see a side by side with one kept open to see what the power output was. Also when the blades are shut they have to open again, there must be a pretty good amount of energy lost while they are opening though perhaps at faster speeds the gravitational forces would keep them open. Which also brings up the point of how weight on those wings matters. Interesting concept, something that you could spend a lot of time tinkering with.

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text:
YeahI thought about this aswell and made a comment about it. There’s definitely some parasitic loss regarding the blades slamming into eachother. 

I was thinking though, would it work even better if you put a very, very soft spring that’s holding the blades closed inwards to possibly prevent some of the parasitic loss coming from the blades hitting and losing energy from the impacts?

replies.author: @zfolwick
replies.text: Conservation of momentum saying that the slamming open will increase the rotation, but on the flip side, the closing slam transfers energy mostly inward instead of backwards, so a net gain compared to fixed blades.  The tradeoff being wear and tear on the machine itself.

replies.author: @karlspranger2468
replies.text:
What about counterweights on the axis of the chord line, inboard and centered about each main blade pivot..
When the wind attempted to open a blade, its opening would be dampened by the static inertia of the counterweight, slowing it down which could be tuned to the average wind speed to prevent blades reaching "full stop" before loosing the wind effects as it turns .. with the added advantage that the counterweights would experience increasing centrifugal force as the rotor speed increased, resisting the blades effort to open even more as rotational velocity increased.

author: @XtomJamesExtra
text: This design is inefficient. It'd be better if it wasn't a flap out design. Too much energy is being lost to the flaps opening and closing thus slowing the rotation of the turbine. It's also very loud. I'd suggest a central screw system which allows you to close the flaps for transport and then open them to a fixed position when in use.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers

replies.author: @XtomJamesExtra
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Don't get me wrong, it's a cool idea, and might be better for a water wheel with a horizontal position, but for wind there simply isn't enough kinetic energy there for the opening and closing process to also transfer the energy tino rotation. The conservation of angular moment is crucial here.

replies.author: @simontemplarGB
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Why not go back to the the Darwin turbine arrangement with the cones directing the wind to a horizontal blade? I understand that you are trying to keep it compact, but perhaps there is a compromise where the cones are in some proportion to the base and even pack with the base. It might be shorter and wider when packed, but give a taller structure with more wind capture whan set up.

author: @xennabrittain-baker9141
text:

text: I'm not a physics guru or something but it seems to me that with the extra moving parts you'd loose quite a bit of the kinetic energy. Just a thought. 😅

author: @DreDPooL13
text: play your video in slow motion and see when it stalls before the next blade hits the stop , the replace the stop at the angle before the stall and i think you will have a better output .

author: @colleenforrest7936
text:
Nice elegant design!

Maybe you could nest a slightly smaller one in the larger one and stack them in layers?

Would that speed up the overall device or slow the device down to the speed of the slowest layer?

Or maybe more loose blades of the same diameter and a top piece inside and you assemble the second layer on sight if needed.  More work for setup though.  Maybe plastidip the blades and hang them to dry on their tabs with the dipped end pointing down for even drying?

text: Brilliant as always, sir. Thank you.

author: @johan96149
text: Fix blades in position halfway, and it will work far better. No need to put blades above generator, leave it in lower position.

author: @Digidoc316
text: As an added bonus, if the winds get too fast, the weight of the blades will cause them to swing outward from enertia and act like a brake, keeping the speed in check.

replies.author: @xmysef4920
replies.text: Damn that’s actually a pretty genius thing, I never thought of that!

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
That's an amazing mod of the previous flat version with raising blades!
I would only put some rubber dampers to reduce that "clack-clack-clack" 😄
After an hour of camping near this, i would go 3 fries short of a happy meal 🤣

author: @margaretwaters7715
text: I am intrigued. I would love this to work in my minivan to help charge my batteries, etc. Keep dreaming Robert ;)

author: @perkins1439
text: If you want to make it quieter put some tiny dabs of silicone on the corners where they touch together and make the clanking sound and let it dry before using it

author: @licence1001011
text: Yes please❤🎉

author: @enigma_7
text:
Awesome build mate. I had a thought about a portable wind generator as I was watching this video. 
Have you tried combining 3d printed parts and fabric? With a 5 gallon bucket filled with water and a tube of silicone caulking and a bedsheet makes a waterproof bedsheet that can be folded and used as the sails for the wind Turbine. It would be easy to carry light and capture alot of wind using fabric sails.

replies.author: @extraincomesuz
replies.text: Laundry flapping in the wind!

author: @azlandpilotcar4450
text: Could your transport rubber band be rigged to cushion the deployed blades from closing completely?  Nice build.  I want to build one for my camper.

author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
I am noticing that your presence is often acting as a bit of a windbreak. Only saying, because some might not notice, and think the device has poor performance.
I'm already trying to think of an assembly line for making the blade assembly out of sheet metal.

replies.author: @Alexander-Herman
replies.text:
You are right, there's a massive energy loss on opening and closing flaps. 
There's a way around this, but it will make the build less simple.
